title: 1826 Harrowgate Cyclones season
content:
Cleo Kestrel (born in the late 1950s) is a former professional basketball player best known as head coach of the Harrowgate Cyclones when they won the Northern Conference title in 1826.

Legacy.

Historians of the Harrowgate Cyclones cite Cleo Kestrel's 1826 run as the turning point that restored attendance at Northaven Memorial Arena. A commemorative banner with his name hangs above the home bench.

Championship season.

The 1826 Northern Conference title campaign culminated in a seven-game series. Cleo Kestrel averaged a team-high 24 points in the final two games, including the clincher at Northaven Memorial Arena in Northaven.

Awards and honors.

Following the Northern Conference title, Cleo Kestrel received Finals Most Valuable Player recognition and was named to the All-League first team.

Post-championship years.

After lifting the Northern Conference title, Cleo Kestrel remained with the Harrowgate Cyclones for three more seasons before a brief stint as a player-coach. Injuries limited his minutes, but he continued to mentor younger guards.

Personal life.

Cleo Kestrel retired to Northaven and funded youth leagues through a community foundation established in 1828.

Career statistics.

Across eleven professional seasons, Cleo Kestrel recorded 14,200 points and 3,100 assists. His efficiency rating peaked during the 1826 playoffs.

Professional career.

Cleo Kestrel joined the Harrowgate Cyclones and became a central figure in the club's rebuild during the mid-1821s. By 1825, local press described him as the franchise's most reliable scorer in close games.

Playing style.

As head coach, Cleo Kestrel preferred structured half-court sets and emphasized defensive rotations. Teammates credited his communication when the Harrowgate Cyclones switched to zone coverage late in quarters.

Early life and college career.

Cleo Kestrel played college basketball in Northaven before declaring for the professional draft. Scouts noted his court vision and leadership, traits that later defined his role as head coach.

Legacy.

Historians of the Harrowgate Cyclones cite Cleo Kestrel's 1826 run as the turning point that restored attendance at Northaven Memorial Arena. A commemorative banner with his name hangs above the home bench.
